metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add either (a) an integration/E2E proving command that runs a plan review on a fixture plan containing a `[rebar:<id>]` citation with a verified edge and asserts the grounding finding is dropped, OR (b) explicitly document in the plan why such an E2E test is impractical (non-determinism) and name any compensating manual acceptance check or deferred child ticket.
evidence: AC #5: 'Rubrics G1G2/E4/E6 carry the `[rebar:<id>]` rule… PROVE: pytest -q tests/unit/plan_review/test_citation_rubric_contract.py'
'The coverage-check is INTENTIONALLY an LLM judgment, NOT a string/path match'
The plan never provides an E2E proving command (e.g. running a plan review on a fixture plan containing a [rebar:] citation and asserting the finding is absent/dropped)
E6-f: 'when the end-to-end exercise is explicitly deferred to a named child with rationale' — no such deferral exists here
findings.finding: The Layer-2 LLM citation-crediting protocol is a live-path behavioral change (it modifies what G1G2/E4/E6 finders do during actual plan reviews against a live model), but the only AC that covers it (test_citation_rubric_contract.py) tests only for the PRESENCE of protocol text in rubric markdown files — a static text check that can pass even if the LLM never actually credits a citation. No end-to-end or integration proving command exercises the live path (a real plan review with a known [rebar:] citation producing a dropped finding), and the plan provides no documented rationale for the absence of such a check.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — AC #5 / Layer 2 section
scenarios: The rubric text is correctly present but the LLM never reads or applies the [rebar:] rule — the AC passes, the behavioral change silently fails
A rubric edit introduces a subtle protocol error (wrong trigger or inputs) that passes text-presence checks but breaks runtime behavior
findings.suggested_fix: Add a prose rationale under Layer 2 such as: 'An automated E2E test is omitted because the crediting decision is non-deterministic; manual acceptance is: run `rebar review <fixture-ticket-id>` against a fixture plan with a known [rebar:C] citation and verified edge, and confirm no G1G2/E4/E6 finding fires for the cited symbol.' Optionally, add this as an operator-attested AC.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (and corresponding test) for the backed-citation happy path in det_floor integration: a PlanContext whose `[rebar:<id>]` citation has a verified edge (depends_on or reverse-blocks) must NOT emit the advisory citation finding.
evidence: AC: 'a PlanContext whose plan has an edge-unbacked [rebar:<id>] yields the advisory citation finding, verdict NOT BLOCK' — covers only the unbacked negative case
Approach Layer 1: 'A citation matching NEITHER (a) nor (b) → an ADVISORY coaching finding' — implies the positive case (matching (a) or (b)) produces no advisory finding, but this is untested by AC
Described deliverable: det_citation is supposed to suppress the advisory signal when an edge is verified — no AC proves this suppression
findings.finding: The 'backed citation → no advisory finding' happy path in det_floor integration has no AC coverage. The AC for det_floor wiring (`test_det_floor_wires_citation`) only specifies the UNBACKED case ('a PlanContext whose plan has an edge-unbacked [rebar:<id>] yields the advisory citation finding'). The complementary case — a PlanContext whose citation IS edge-verified → advisory finding is NOT emitted — is described in the Approach ('A citation matching NEITHER (a) nor (b) → an ADVISORY coaching finding') but has no proving command.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — AC line 4 (`test_det_floor_wires_citation`) and Approach § Layer 1
scenarios: An implementer might accidentally emit an advisory finding for ALL citations (including backed ones); the existing ACs would not catch this regression.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC: `- [ ] WIRING (happy path): a PlanContext whose plan has an edge-BACKED [rebar:<id>]` citation yields NO advisory citation finding. PROVE: `pytest -q tests/unit/plan_review/test_det_citation.py::test_det_floor_backed_citation_silent`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend `test_parse`) to assert that `det_citation` exports an `accruing_since` constant conforming to the existing reader-module convention.
evidence: Plan quote: 'accruing_since for any emitted signal is a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules'
No AC line mentions accruing_since or verifies the module conforms to the reader-module convention
findings.finding: The `accruing_since` module-level constant is described as a structural deliverable of `det_citation.py` ('accruing_since for any emitted signal is a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules') but no AC verifies its presence or correct value.
findings.location: Approach § Layer 1 / Files — src/rebar/llm/plan_review/det_citation.py
scenarios: An implementer could omit the constant or set it to an arbitrary value; the test suite would not catch the deviation from the reader-module convention.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the `test_parse` AC or add a dedicated assertion: `assert isinstance(det_citation.accruing_since, str) and det_citation.accruing_since <= <some_reference_module>.accruing_since`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or AGENTS.md section capturing the two-layer citation-verification architecture, the `[rebar:<id>]` grammar contract, and the fail-open (Layer 1 advisory) / fail-closed (Layer 2 LLM) split as a persistent design artifact.
evidence: 'Rubric SoT: src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_{G1G2,E4,E6}.md' — rubrics document the LLM protocol only, not the two-layer architecture or grammar contract
ABSENCE: no ADR, AGENTS.md, or design-doc update appears in 'Files likely touched' or 'Scope (IN)'
The Approach & Rationale section is detailed but lives in the ticket description, not a persistent design artifact
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel two-layer citation-verification architecture and a new grammar contract (`[rebar:<id>]`) but does not include an ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc entry capturing this architectural decision for future maintainers of det_citation.py and det_floor.py.
findings.location: Scope section / Files likely touched
scenarios: A future contributor modifying det_floor.py or det_citation.py has no persistent design document explaining WHY citations are verified in two layers, why the grammar is structured as it is, or what the fail-open/fail-closed split is and where to find it.
If the grammar is extended (e.g., transitive closure, explicitly listed as OUT), the absence of a design doc makes it unclear which extension points are intentional vs. accidental.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `docs/adr/citation-verification-two-layer.md` (or an AGENTS.md section) covering: (1) grammar definition and parser location, (2) Layer 1 advisory-only rationale, (3) Layer 2 fail-closed LLM protocol, (4) explicit out-of-scope items (transitive closure, snapshot-pinning). Reference it from det_citation.py's module docstring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge the det_floor.py LOC constraint explicitly: either confirm the advisory-lane wiring fits within the remaining ~54 lines, or add a contingency (e.g., extract the advisory-lane dispatch into a `det_floor_advisory.py` helper) if the addition exceeds capacity.
evidence: 'det_floor is 746/800 LOC' — explicitly noted, implying a known file-size constraint
'small addition' — asserted without quantifying the addition or stating what happens if the limit is exceeded
No extraction, refactoring, or module-split is mentioned as a mitigation
findings.finding: det_floor.py is noted as 746/800 LOC (93% capacity), and the plan proposes adding advisory-lane wiring for det_citation without a mitigation plan if that addition pushes the module over its LOC limit or makes it harder to maintain.
findings.location: Files likely touched — 'det_floor.py (advisory-lane wiring; supply resolve_deps backed by _reads.show_ticket; small addition — det_floor is 746/800 LOC)'
scenarios: The 'small addition' turns out to require more LOC than anticipated (e.g., resolve_deps plumbing, error handling for missing tickets), pushing det_floor.py over 800 LOC and triggering linter/reviewer warnings or a forced emergency refactor mid-implementation.
Future changes to the advisory lane (e.g., adding another advisory checker) find det_floor already at capacity, creating a growing maintenance burden.
findings.suggested_fix: Quantify the expected LOC addition in the plan (e.g., 'estimated +20 lines for advisory-lane hook and resolve_deps factory'). If uncertain, add a contingency: 'if addition exceeds available LOC, extract advisory dispatcher to det_floor_advisory.py and import it.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `det_citation` wraps every `resolve_deps` call in error handling (try/except) and, on any exception or unexpected response, treats the cited edge as **unverified** (emitting at most an advisory warning about the lookup failure) rather than propagating the exception — honouring the fail-open / never-blocks contract at the implementation level.
evidence: 'det_floor supplies one backed by `_reads.show_ticket`' — no error-handling contract specified
'one read per distinct cited id' — each read is a new external I/O call
Plan asserts 'never blocks (respects fail-open floor)' but does not specify that resolve_deps errors are caught and treated as 'unverified' to honour fail-open
ABSENCE: no mention of try/except, timeout, or graceful-degradation path for the show_ticket reverse-lookup call
findings.finding: The `resolve_deps` callable in `det_citation.py` (backed by `_reads.show_ticket`) is an external I/O call with no specified error handling: if `show_ticket` throws, times out, or returns an unexpected structure for a cited ticket id, the plan does not define whether det_citation catches the exception and degrades gracefully (treating the edge as unverified) or propagates the exception up through the advisory lane, potentially aborting the overall det_floor run.
findings.location: Layer 1 — det_citation.py / resolve_deps callable; Approach & rationale section; AC test_det_floor_wires_citation
scenarios: show_ticket raises a network/timeout error for a cited ticket id → uncaught exception propagates through det_citation → advisory lane crashes → det_floor run aborted or verdict corrupted
cited ticket id is valid but show_ticket returns a malformed/empty deps list → KeyError or AttributeError in edge-check logic → same propagation failure
findings.suggested_fix: In the det_citation spec, add an explicit error contract: `resolve_deps(id) -> list[dep]` implementors MUST NOT raise; or equivalently, det_citation wraps the call in `try/except Exception` and on any error returns an empty dep list (edge treated as unverified) plus logs a debug-level warning. The AC for `test_det_floor_wires_citation` should include a subcase where resolve_deps raises and confirm the verdict is still NOT BLOCK and a graceful advisory finding (or no finding) is returned.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add test cases in `test_det_citation.py::test_parse` (or a sibling) for: empty plan text (returns `[]`), malformed ids (`[rebar:]`, `[rebar:a b]`), unclosed bracket, and multiple citations in one plan — verify each returns the expected result or is skipped/rejected per the grammar contract.
evidence: AC reads: '`det_citation.parse_citations(plan_text)` returns `(subject, ticket_id)` per `[rebar:<id>]`. PROVE: … test_parse.' — only happy-path described.
Grammar section says `<ticket-id>` matches rebar's id/alias grammar but no AC verifies rejection/handling of inputs that do not match.
ABSENCE: no AC for empty plan text, zero citations, or malformed bracket variants.
findings.finding: The `test_parse` AC covers only the happy path (well-formed `[rebar:<id>]` tokens), with no specified tests for malformed citation syntax — e.g. `[rebar:]` (empty id), `[rebar:a b]` (spaces in id), unclosed bracket, or a plan with zero citations. `parse_citations` is new conditional logic with a grammar contract; silently returning wrong results on malformed input can cause Layer-1 edge verification to skip real citations or manufacture spurious ones.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — `pytest … test_det_citation.py::test_parse`
scenarios: A plan author makes a typo (`[rebar:]` or `[rebar:foo bar]`); the parser silently drops or misparses the citation; Layer-1 gives no advisory signal; the author never learns the citation was ignored.
A plan has zero `[rebar:…]` tokens; test_parse never runs the empty-input code path; a future refactor breaks the empty-return case undetected.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `test_parse` with parametrized cases: `("", [])`, `("foo [rebar:]", [])`, `("foo [rebar:a b]", [])`, `("foo [rebar:T-1] bar [rebar:T-2]", [("foo", "T-1"), ("bar", "T-2")])`. Define in the grammar section whether non-matching brackets are silently skipped (recommended) and assert that behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test case (e.g. `test_det_citation.py::test_resolve_deps_error_stays_advisory`) asserting that when `resolve_deps` raises or returns empty, `det_citation` either emits an advisory finding or skips gracefully — and that the det_floor wiring never propagates the exception as a BLOCK verdict.
evidence: Plan states det_floor 'supply resolve_deps backed by `_reads.show_ticket`' — a live I/O call.
AC `test_det_floor_wires_citation` checks only the success path (edge-unbacked citation → advisory finding, verdict NOT BLOCK).
ABSENCE: no AC for resolve_deps raising / returning empty — no assertion that the floor still reaches a non-BLOCK verdict in the error case.
Plan says 'never blocks (respects fail-open floor)' but no test enforces this for the error path.
findings.finding: No AC specifies the behavior of the advisory lane when the injected `resolve_deps` callable fails (raises an exception, returns an empty list, or is unavailable because `_reads.show_ticket` hits a network/ticket-not-found error). The plan requires a live I/O call per distinct cited id, so transient failures are plausible. Without a specified failure path, an implementation might propagate the exception out of the advisory lane and inadvertently affect the floor's overall verdict — violating the fail-open principle the plan explicitly depends on.
findings.location: Layer 1 description — 'bounded reverse lookup (one read per distinct cited id)'; Acceptance criteria — `test_det_floor_wires_citation`
scenarios: CI environment loses connectivity; `_reads.show_ticket` throws; unguarded exception propagates from det_citation through det_floor's advisory lane; floor verdict is unexpectedly affected or the entire review errors out.

Ticket id cited in plan does not exist yet (normal during active development); show_ticket returns 404/empty; advisory lane silently swallows the miss and emits no finding, making it look like the citation was verified when it wasn't.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that det_citation.py's standalone extraction is justified by the det_floor size ceiling alone (acceptable if so — state this explicitly) OR identify a second planned consumer (e.g. a workflow_ops re-export for completion-verifier citation awareness) that would satisfy the Rule-of-Three precedent set by det_operator_attested.
evidence: Plan: 'reuses the det_operator_attested inline-tag PARSING approach … genuinely mirrors det_operator_attested, which never blocks'
det_operator_attested.py docstring: '_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE is the single source workflow_ops re-exports' — confirming workflow_ops is a second independent consumer
workflow_ops.py:36: 'from rebar.llm.plan_review.det_operator_attested import (_OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE as _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_AC_RE)' — the second call-site that justified extraction
Plan's Files section names only det_floor.py as a consumer of det_citation; no workflow_ops or other second consumer is planned

det_lint.py docstring: 'Extracted from det_floor … so that grandfathered, size-ceilinged module does not grow' — size ceiling is the stated extraction rationale, but det_lint also has multiple consumers
findings.finding: det_citation.py is proposed as a standalone module 'mirroring det_operator_attested', but at delivery it will have only 1 production call-site (det_floor advisory lane). det_operator_attested's extraction was Rule-of-Three-justified because workflow_ops constitutes a second independent consumer (re-exporting _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE for the completion-verifier enrichment path). No equivalent second consumer of det_citation is planned, so the module extraction is premature by the project's own precedent.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — Layer 1 / Files likely touched: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/det_citation.py
scenarios: If the module remains single-consumer indefinitely, the extraction added a module boundary, an injected-callable API, and a separate test file for logic that could have lived inline in det_floor.p6_ac_quality (as the operator-attested issues list does for its first iteration).
If a future second consumer (e.g. a workflow_ops citation-tag re-export for completion verification) is intended but unstated, the plan underspecifies scope.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) add a sentence to the plan explicitly accepting single-consumer extraction on size-ceiling grounds (same rationale as det_lint.py), or (b) name a second consumer (e.g. note that workflow_ops will re-export the [rebar:] tag regex for completion-verifier use, analogous to _OPERATOR_ATTESTED_TAG_RE) — whichever matches the actual intent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether `accruing_since` is an established module-level constant pattern in any existing det_* reader module (a Grep confirms it is absent); either remove the claim or identify the actual existing convention being mirrored so the implementation contract is grounded.
evidence: Plan text: 'accruing_since for any emitted signal is a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules'
Search for 'accruing_since' in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ returns zero hits
det_operator_attested.py (the named mirror) contains no such constant — confirmed by reading the file
The assertion claims parity with existing reader modules but no reader module defines this constant
findings.finding: The plan asserts that `det_citation.py` will have an `accruing_since` module-level constant 'matching the existing reader modules', implying such a constant is a pattern in the module it mirrors (det_operator_attested). A full search of `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/det_operator_attested.py` and the entire `plan_review` directory finds zero occurrences of `accruing_since`. This pattern does not exist in the claimed mirror module or anywhere in the det floor, making the assertion unverifiable and the implementation contract underdefined.
findings.location: Layer 1 — det_citation.py description: 'accruing_since for any emitted signal is a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules'
scenarios: An implementer looks for the `accruing_since` pattern in det_operator_attested to copy it and finds nothing — the spec is orphaned.

The AC test (test_det_citation.py) that verifies the module shape may or may not test for accruing_since; if the pattern is fake, a test checking for it will fail, and if it's omitted, the spec quietly diverges.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `accruing_since` clause from the Layer 1 description, or replace it with the actual pattern used in det_operator_attested.py (e.g., no such constant — the module simply defines its functions and the p6 entry-point calls them).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the test file paths to match the existing flat layout (`tests/unit/test_det_citation.py`, `tests/unit/test_citation_rubric_contract.py`) and update all AC prove commands accordingly, OR explicitly add a note that the implementer should create the `tests/unit/plan_review/` subdirectory as part of this ticket.
evidence: Plan names: tests/unit/plan_review/test_det_citation.py and tests/unit/plan_review/test_citation_rubric_contract.py

Filesystem search confirms NO tests/unit/plan_review/ directory exists; all plan-review unit tests sit directly under tests/unit/ (e.g. test_det_floor_operator_attested.py, test_plan_review.py, test_a8e5_det_hygiene_edge.py)
AC prove command: 'pytest -q tests/unit/plan_review/test_det_citation.py::test_parse' — path does not exist
findings.finding: The two new test files are placed under tests/unit/plan_review/ (a subdirectory), but the existing test layout is flat under tests/unit/ — no tests/unit/plan_review/ directory exists. Every AC 'PROVE' command references this non-existent path, so pytest will fail with a path-collection error before running any test.
findings.location: Files likely touched / AC prove commands (test_det_citation.py, test_citation_rubric_contract.py)
scenarios: Implementer creates the new test files at the plan-specified path and every pytest invocation in the AC fails with 'ERROR: not found: .../tests/unit/plan_review/test_det_citation.py'
CI runs the regenerate + suite-green command 'pytest -q tests/unit/plan_review -k ...' and errors on directory not found
findings.suggested_fix: Change the file paths to `tests/unit/test_det_citation.py` and `tests/unit/test_citation_rubric_contract.py` (matching peer files like `tests/unit/test_det_floor_operator_attested.py`) and update all five `pytest -q tests/unit/plan_review/…` commands to `pytest -q tests/unit/test_det_citation.py::…` and `pytest -q tests/unit/test_citation_rubric_contract.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove or correct the `accruing_since` reference: either drop the claim (no such convention exists), or define the exact field name, type, and location in the DetResult/finding dict schema that the implementer should add.
evidence: Full codebase search for 'accruing_since' returns zero matches
det_operator_attested.py (the cited analog module) contains no accruing_since constant — confirmed by reading the full file
det_floor.py DetResult dataclass has no accruing_since field
findings.finding: The plan asserts that det_citation.py should carry an `accruing_since` module-level constant 'matching the existing reader modules', but no such constant exists anywhere in the codebase — not in det_operator_attested.py (the cited analog), not in det_floor.py, not in any other plan_review module. The convention is fabricated.
findings.location: Layer 1 description: 'accruing_since for any emitted signal is a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules'
scenarios: Implementer wastes time searching for the 'existing reader module' convention to match and finds nothing, or invents an incompatible interface
If accruing_since is threaded into DetResult or the advisory finding dict, it will be an unrecognised key that downstream consumers (orchestrator, sidecar) silently ignore or reject

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `resolve_deps` callable supplied by det_floor must pass `repo_root=ctx.tickets_root` (not `repo_root` / bare) to `_reads.show_ticket`, matching the established pattern at `pass1.py:319`.
evidence: Plan text: 'det_floor supplies one backed by _reads.show_ticket'
pass1.py:319: `log = _reads.show_ticket(tgt, repo_root=ctx.tickets_root)` — existing precedent always passes tickets_root
det_floor.py:126: `tickets_root: str | None = None` — field exists on PlanContext specifically for this purpose
orchestrator.py:208: `tickets_root=current_tickets_root()` — set during attested context assembly
findings.finding: The plan specifies that det_floor supplies a `resolve_deps` callable 'backed by `_reads.show_ticket`' but does not specify that the call must pass `repo_root=ctx.tickets_root`. In an attested-gate context `tickets_root` is non-None and distinct from `repo_root` (the code root); all existing DET-floor callsites that read prerequisite tickets (e.g. `pass1.py:319`) consistently thread `ctx.tickets_root` through. A `resolve_deps` that calls `_reads.show_ticket(id)` without `repo_root=ctx.tickets_root` would silently read from the wrong store in attested runs, causing genuine dependency edges to be unresolvable and valid citations to be incorrectly reported as edge-unbacked.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — Layer 1 / 'det_floor supplies one backed by _reads.show_ticket' / Files likely touched: det_floor.py
scenarios: Running the plan-review gate in attested mode (on a CI snapshot where the ticket store is on the orphan `tickets` branch): resolve_deps reads from the code snapshot root, finds no `.tickets-tracker`, returns an error or empty deps — so every cited dep is treated as edge-unbacked and the advisory finding fires incorrectly.

A citation `foo/bar.py [rebar:abc1]` where abc1 genuinely has a `blocks->P` edge: the reverse lookup fails silently due to wrong root, the citation is flagged as unverified, and the advisory noise the feature was meant to suppress appears anyway.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Layer 1 spec, add: 'det_floor constructs `resolve_deps` as `lambda ticket_id: _reads.show_ticket(ticket_id, repo_root=ctx.tickets_root).get("deps", []) or []`' — mirroring the `pass1.py:319` pattern. The AC for `test_det_floor_wires_citation` should include an attested-context fixture where `tickets_root` differs from `repo_root` to catch this regression.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `det_citation.py` actually needs an `accruing_since` constant (no such pattern exists in any current `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/` DET module — it is a `src/rebar/metrics/` concept); if it is needed, name the concrete consumer; if not, remove the assertion from the plan.
evidence: Search for 'accruing_since' in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ returns zero matches.
Search for '_ACCRUING_SINCE' in src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ returns zero matches.
det_operator_attested.py (the stated structural template) has no accruing_since constant.
accruing_since is defined and used in src/rebar/metrics/registry.py (MetricSpec.accruing_since: str) — a different subsystem entirely.
Plan states: 'accruing_since for any emitted signal is a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules' — but no reader module in the plan_review package has this field.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that det_citation.py should carry 'a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules' for accruing_since, but no such constant exists in any current src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ module — the accruing_since pattern lives in src/rebar/metrics/, not in the plan_review DET layer. This is an unverified capability claim about a pattern that does not exist in the target package.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — Layer 1: '`accruing_since` for any emitted signal is a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules'
scenarios: Implementer adds a spurious accruing_since constant that has no consumer (dead code, silently ignored).
Implementer omits it and the AC test (test_det_citation.py) fails unexpectedly because the plan's API contract is wrong.
A code reviewer flags the constant as misplaced; the implementation stalls.
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the `accruing_since` reference from the Layer 1 description, or add a sentence naming the exact consumer (e.g. a metrics spec) that will read it — otherwise an implementer has no grounded reason to include it.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the output schema the LLM finder must emit when evaluating a `[rebar:<C>]` citation (e.g., a `citation_credited: bool` or `citation_verdict: 'credited'|'not-credited'` field co-located with the coverage-check protocol in the G1G2/E4/E6 rubric edits), so the 'affirmatively confirms' decision boundary is machine-parseable.
evidence: Plan states: 'credit the citation — treat the symbol as EXISTING and DROP the finding — ONLY if the reviewer affirmatively confirms C provides that functionality. If C does not, or the reviewer is uncertain, the finding STANDS.'

No output schema (binary YES/NO field, confidence key, structured JSON) is specified for the rubric edits to G1G2/E4/E6 that would make 'affirmatively confirms' machine-parseable or consistently reproducible across reviewer invocations.

Existing rubrics (plan_review_G1G2.md, plan_review_E4.md, plan_review_E6.md — read and confirmed) use prose decision rules without a structured output schema, so an implementer has no prior pattern to follow for the credit/no-credit signal.
findings.finding: The Layer-2 'affirmatively confirms' decision output is undefined: the rubric edits to G1G2/E4/E6 are supposed to encode the LLM coverage protocol, but the plan never specifies what output token/structure the LLM finder must produce to signal 'affirmed' vs 'uncertain', leaving the decision boundary undefined for an implementer writing those rubric files.
findings.location: Layer 2 — LLM agent grounding / 'DECISION (FAIL-CLOSED)' paragraph; also the rubric-edit scope items for plan_review_{G1G2,E4,E6}.md
scenarios: An implementer edits plan_review_G1G2.md and writes 'if the ticket provides the functionality, credit it' — but without a defined output structure, two different LLM runs may produce inconsistent drop/keep decisions on the same citation.
Pass-2/Pass-3 logic that consumes the finding list has no reliable signal to distinguish 'dropped because citation credited' from 'dropped for other reasons', making the feature unauditable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a small structured output section to each rubric edit: 'When evaluating a `[rebar:<C>]` citation, record your conclusion as `citation_verdict: credited` (drop the finding) or `citation_verdict: not-credited` (finding stands). Default to `not-credited` on any uncertainty.' This gives the fail-closed contract a concrete shape.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either define `accruing_since` concretely in the plan (specify its type, its value, and point to an existing module that uses the same pattern) or remove the reference if it is vestigial.
evidence: search_files for 'accruing_since' across src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ returns zero matches.
search_files for 'accruing' across src/rebar/llm/plan_review/ returns zero matches.
The plan references 'existing reader modules' as the source of this pattern, but no such constant is present in det_operator_attested.py or det_floor.py (both read in full).
An implementer writing det_citation.py cannot determine what value to assign to this constant or what type it should be.
findings.finding: `accruing_since` is referenced as a module-level constant for `det_citation.py` that 'matches the existing reader modules', but this symbol does not exist anywhere in the `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/` codebase — it is an undefined enum/constant whose value, type, and sourcing pattern are all unspecified.
findings.location: Layer 1 — deterministic, ADVISORY section, last sentence: '`accruing_since` for any emitted signal is a module-level constant matching the existing reader modules.'
scenarios: Developer writes `det_citation.py`, hits the `accruing_since` instruction, cannot find any existing example in the codebase, and either omits it (breaking the contract with the advisory lane consumer) or invents a value (potentially causing downstream issues).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the error-handling contract for `resolve_deps`: define whether a failed/raising call (ticket not found, store error) should be treated as 'unbacked citation' (advisory finding stands, fail-closed) or 'abstain' (no advisory emitted), and document the expected return type on error (`[]` vs exception propagation).
evidence: Plan states: 'takes P's deps PLUS an injected `resolve_deps(ticket_id) -> list[dep]` callable (det_floor supplies one backed by `_reads.show_ticket`) to do the bounded reverse lookup (one read per distinct cited id).'
Existing show_ticket usage in pass1.py:319-321 wraps each call in try/except with 'continue' on error (best-effort).
Existing show_ticket usage in orchestrator.py:184-187 also wraps per-child reads in try/except with fallback.

No fallback is described for `resolve_deps` raising or returning empty/malformed data — the AC test `test_edge_both_directions` covers the happy path but the plan does not specify whether a failed reverse-lookup should count as 'unbacked' (safe, fail-closed) or 'abstain'.
findings.finding: The `resolve_deps` callable's error-handling contract is unspecified: the plan says 'det_floor supplies one backed by `_reads.show_ticket`' for the reverse-lookup, but does not specify what `det_citation` should do when the `show_ticket` call for a cited ticket raises (ticket not found, store read error) — a gap since the existing codebase uses per-call best-effort try/except uniformly for all show_ticket reads.
findings.location: Layer 1 — 'det_citation therefore takes P's deps PLUS an injected `resolve_deps(ticket_id) -> list[dep]` callable'; also the AC line 'Edge-check verifies BOTH directions and rejects downstream'
scenarios: A cited ticket C exists in the plan text but has been deleted or is in a different ticket store — `resolve_deps(C)` raises; without a specified fallback, det_citation could either swallow the error silently (treating the citation as backed) or crash (abstaining the whole det check).
The injected callable's return type on error is unspecified — a caller returning `None` vs `[]` vs raising produces different behavior in det_citation's edge-check logic.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Layer-1 spec: 'A `resolve_deps` call that raises or returns an empty/malformed result is treated as "no upstream edge found" — the citation is unbacked and the advisory finding is emitted. The callable must never raise; det_floor's implementation wraps `_reads.show_ticket` in a try/except returning `[]` on any error.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a subject-to-citation matching rule to the Layer-2 TRIGGER spec (e.g., 'evaluate ALL `[rebar:<C>]` tokens that appear within the same sentence or bullet as the flagged subject; credit if ANY verified citation provides the functionality; if none, the finding stands') so the trigger boundary is unambiguous for rubric implementers.
evidence: Plan states: 'TRIGGER: a grounding finder (G1G2/E4/E6) is about to flag a symbol/functionality as missing, and that symbol is cited `<subject> [rebar:<C>]` with an edge VERIFIED by Layer 1.'
Plan states: 'the coverage-check is INTENTIONALLY an LLM judgment, NOT a string/path match — an exact matching rule is impractical because relied-upon functionality is described in prose.'

No heuristic is given for resolving ambiguity when a plan paragraph contains multiple `[rebar:<C1>]` and `[rebar:<C2>]` tokens and the flagged symbol is near both — which citation(s) does the finder evaluate?
The Grammar section defines `<subject>` as 'free text naming the relied-upon code element' but does not define how the finder identifies this subject in its own flagging context.
findings.finding: The Layer-2 trigger's subject-to-citation matching rule is underspecified: when a grounding finder flags a symbol as missing, the plan provides no rule for how the finder determines which `[rebar:<C>]` token in the plan text corresponds to the flagged symbol — leaving the trigger condition ambiguous when multiple citations appear near a flagged element.
findings.location: Layer 2 — TRIGGER condition: 'a grounding finder (G1G2/E4/E6) is about to flag a symbol/functionality as missing, and that symbol is cited `<subject> [rebar:<C>]` with an edge VERIFIED by Layer 1'
scenarios: A plan references two separate tickets near a missing symbol: 'SymbolA [rebar:abc1] and SymbolB [rebar:def2] will both be created'. Finder flags SymbolA — should it check abc1, def2, or both? No rule is given.
A plan has a dense paragraph with three `[rebar:]` citations; a finder flagging a general 'module X is missing' claim cannot determine which citation applies without a defined matching heuristic.

findings.finding: No acceptance criterion verifies that `det_citation` is actually wired into `det_floor.py`'s advisory lane. `det_floor.py` is listed in 'Files likely touched' and the Approach says 'wired into det_floor's advisory coverage,' but all test ACs only exercise `det_citation` in isolation. An executor could ship passing unit tests while forgetting to add the call in `det_floor.py`, and the suite would still go green.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria section; 'Files likely touched' → det_floor.py
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to ACs:
```
- [ ] `det_floor` surfaces a det_citation advisory finding when a plan contains a `[rebar:<id>]` citation whose id has no direct upstream edge in `ctx.state['deps']`. PROVE: `pytest -q tests/unit/plan_review/test_det_citation.py::test_det_floor_wires_citation_advisory`
```
The new test calls `det_floor.run(ctx)` with a synthetic `PlanContext` whose plan_text includes `some_module.py [rebar:T-999]` and whose `deps` list contains no edge to `T-999`, then asserts the returned findings include at least one advisory entry from `det_citation`.

findings.finding: The `unbacked_citations` edge-check definition conflates edge direction for the `blocks` relation. In `ctx.state['deps']`, an entry with `relation='blocks'` means the CURRENT ticket blocks the TARGET (the target is DOWNSTREAM, not a prerequisite). The plan says 'blocks whose source is the id' — but in the deps list the source is always the current ticket, so a `blocks` entry means the cited id is downstream, not upstream. Accepting this as a verified direct prerequisite would let a citation to a DOWNSTREAM ticket bypass the missing-symbol check.
findings.location: Approach & rationale — LAYER 1 paragraph: 'depends_on whose target is the id, or blocks whose source is the id'
findings.suggested_fix: In det_citation.unbacked_citations, accept only deps where `dep['relation'] == 'depends_on' and dep['target_id'] == cited_id`. Mirror the same fix in the Layer-2 rubric language: replace 'depends_on-target / blocks-source' with 'the ticket under review carries a depends_on edge whose target is <cited-id>'.

findings.finding: The Layer-2 coverage-check protocol for `show_ticket` results is underspecified: the plan says the agent confirms the cited ticket's `file_impact` 'contains the file that declares the symbol OR its plan/ACs name the symbol as a created artifact,' but no matching rule is given (exact path match? substring? symbol name appearance in freeform description text?) and no fallback is stated for when `show_ticket` returns an error string (the tool returns `'Error: ...'` on failure per `pai_tools.py:213`). The rubric edits to G1G2/E4/E6.md — which is where the agent reads its decision rules — must co-locate this protocol, but the plan does not specify it precisely enough to write those rubric lines.
findings.location: Plan section 'Approach & rationale' — LAYER 2; files src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_{G1G2,E4,E6}.md (to be modified)
findings.suggested_fix: In the rubric additions to G1G2/E4/E6.md, add: 'Coverage confirmed iff (a) `show_ticket` returns a valid JSON state AND (b) the cited file path appears verbatim in `file_impact[].path` OR the cited symbol name appears as a code-formatted token (backtick-quoted) in the plan/description/ACs text. On any `show_ticket` error or no-match, the citation is NOT backed and the symbol is treated as missing.'

findings.finding: The `det_citation` direct-edge check for the `blocks` direction is logically inverted. The plan states: 'depends_on whose target is the id, OR blocks whose source is the id.' In `ctx.state['deps']`, each dep has `{target_id, relation}` from the perspective of the CURRENT ticket. A dep with `relation='blocks'` and `target_id=cited_id` means THIS ticket blocks the cited ticket — i.e., the cited ticket depends on this one, which is the WRONG direction for an upstream prerequisite. A valid upstream edge from cited_id to the current ticket appears as `relation='depends_on', target_id=cited_id` in the current ticket's deps. The `blocks`-direction clause as written checks the inverse edge and will never match a valid upstream citation.
findings.location: Plan section 'Approach & rationale' — LAYER 1 (det_citation.py); specifically `unbacked_citations(citations, deps)` logic
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'depends_on whose target is the id, OR blocks whose source is the id' with 'a dep entry in ctx.state["deps"] where relation=="depends_on" and target_id==cited_id' — this is the one unambiguous upstream-prerequisite representation in rebar's dep schema, consistent with how det_floor.py p5_task_dag already processes the same data.

findings.finding: The Layer-2 'coverage-check' protocol — what the agent must confirm inside the show_ticket response to treat a cited symbol as EXISTING — is never defined. The rubric edits are described only as 'an edge-verified [rebar:<id>]-cited symbol is grounded via a show_ticket coverage-check', but no protocol is specified: does the finder search the cited ticket's description, file_impact, title, or plan for the symbol name? Without this rule co-located with the rubric schema, different agents will apply different heuristics, producing inconsistent grounding results.
findings.location: Plan section 'Approach & rationale (G6)' — LAYER 2; also plan section 'Acceptance criteria' AC item for G1G2/E4/E6 rubrics
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'CITATION COVERAGE PROTOCOL' block to each updated rubric (G1G2/E4): 'After show_ticket(<id>), a symbol S is COVERED if S appears (exact or qualified) in the cited ticket's description/plan body OR in a file_impact entry path whose basename matches S's module. If neither, the citation is UNBACKED and the symbol remains ungrounded.'

findings.finding: det_floor.py wiring (calling det_citation in the advisory lane) is a named deliverable in both the Approach and Files sections, but no AC proves the integration actually happens. All ACs target det_citation module functions in isolation (test_det_citation.py) and rubric text (test_citation_rubric_contract.py); nothing verifies det_floor.py emits det_citation findings during a real advisory pass.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria section; Files: 'src/rebar/llm/plan_review/det_floor.py (call det_citation in the advisory coverage lane)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a new AC: '`det_floor` emits a det_citation advisory finding when a plan contains an unbacked `[rebar:<id>]` token. PROVE: `pytest -q tests/unit/plan_review/test_det_citation.py::test_det_floor_wiring`' and add the corresponding test that calls `det_floor.check(ctx)` with a plan containing an unbacked citation.

findings.finding: All three `det_citation` ACs are pure unit tests on the new module in isolation. There is no AC that exercises the `det_floor` wiring end-to-end — i.e., that a plan text containing a `[rebar:<id>]` citation passed through the full `det_floor` path actually emits an advisory finding in the floor's output (and does not accidentally change the verdict). The `test_advisory_never_blocks` test proves det_citation itself is advisory but cannot catch a wiring bug in `det_floor.py` that drops the advisory result or, conversely, one that accidentally promotes it to a block.
findings.location: Acceptance criteria — third AC (`test_advisory_never_blocks`) and Files likely touched — `det_floor.py`
findings.suggested_fix: Add a test in `test_det_citation.py` or a new `test_det_floor_citation_wiring.py` that constructs a minimal `PlanContext` with a plan containing `[rebar:X]` (X not in deps) and calls the det_floor entry point, asserting the advisory finding is present in findings and verdict != BLOCK.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
